name: Windows Host Overview (Conduit default)
description: Default board for the Conduit windows profile (M6.D). Two halves: per-host resource pressure (broken down by host.name; CPU Queue Length is the Windows-native saturation indicator that replaces Unix load average) and a Windows Event Log narrative organized around the channels and providers a Windows admin actually queries. Filter by `Host` to scope to a single machine.

panels.content:
# Windows Host Overview

Default platform telemetry from **Conduit's windows profile** ([`internal/profiles/windows/`](https://github.com/conduit-obs/conduit-agent/tree/main/internal/profiles/windows)). The board has two halves:

| Section | Source | Primary key | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Resource pressure** | `metrics` (hostmetrics receiver) | `host.name` | CPU Queue Length is the Windows-native saturation indicator emitted as `system.cpu.load_average.1m` (the `load` scraper on Windows reads the `System\Processor Queue Length` performance counter; there is no 5m / 15m equivalent). CPU / memory / filesystem **utilization** panels mirror the linux-host-overview board because the underlying telemetry shape is identical — same scrapers, same `*.utilization` column names. |
| **Windows Event Log** | `windows-host` (windowseventlog/{application,system}) | `winlog.channel` + `winlog.provider_name` | Each event arrives with `winlog.channel`, `winlog.provider_name`, `winlog.event_id`, `winlog.task`, plus `severity_text` mapped from the Event level (Critical→FATAL, Error→ERROR, Warning→WARN, Information→INFO, Verbose→DEBUG). The narrative is organized around channels and providers — the Windows admin's mental model — rather than filelog-style template matching, which doesn't fit Event Log data. |

**Security channel** is intentionally not loaded by default — it requires `SeSecurityPrivilege` (running as `LocalSystem` or a member of `Event Log Readers` with Group Policy support). Sites that need it add a Security receiver via the [`overrides:` escape hatch](https://github.com/conduit-obs/conduit-agent/blob/main/docs/adr/adr-0012.md) and arrange the privilege grant in their MSI install.

**Page File** maps to `system.paging.utilization` — the same metric name as Linux swap, so cross-platform paging-pressure dashboards work without extra mapping. Sustained `state=used` above 0.5 typically indicates RAM pressure that's spilling to the page file.

For the cross-platform telemetry-shape contract this board satisfies, see [`internal/profiles/PROFILE_SPEC.md`](https://github.com/conduit-obs/conduit-agent/blob/main/internal/profiles/PROFILE_SPEC.md).

_Dashboard generated by Conduit V0 MVP. Stored as a checked-in definition under `dashboards/` in the Conduit repo._

panels.name: CPU Queue Length (saturation) by host
panels.description: AVG of system.cpu.load_average.1m by host.name. On Windows this is the System\Processor Queue Length performance counter — the count of threads ready to run but waiting on a logical CPU. Sustained values above core-count indicate saturated CPUs; a single brief spike during a build / GC pause is normal and not actionable.

panels.name: CPU Utilization (% non-idle) by state, per host
panels.description: AVG of system.cpu.utilization (0..1 fraction) by host.name + state, with state=idle filtered out. Windows reports user / system / idle states; sustained system>0.5 with growing queue length is a good signal that the kernel is spending CPU on I/O or interrupt handling rather than user code.

panels.name: Memory Utilization by state, per host
panels.description: AVG of system.memory.utilization (0..1 fraction) by host.name + state. Windows reports `used` and `free`; the receiver doesn't break out the cache / standby pools that Task Manager surfaces, so this panel is best read alongside the Page File panel below — high page-file usage with low free memory is the classic 'committed memory exceeds RAM' pattern.

panels.name: Page File Utilization, per host
panels.description: AVG of system.paging.utilization (0..1 fraction). Windows uses page files (typically pagefile.sys on the system drive) for committed-memory backing; sustained `state=used` rising while system.memory.utilization stays flat is the classic sign that working sets are spilling to disk.

panels.name: Filesystem Utilization (% used) by volume, per host
panels.description: MAX of system.filesystem.utilization (0..1 fraction) restricted to state=used. Windows reports each fixed volume (C:, D:, ...) under the device attribute; CD-ROM and network shares are excluded by the receiver. Low-free-space alarms here typically map to log directories under %PROGRAMDATA% or developer-tool caches under %LOCALAPPDATA%.

panels.name: Disk I/O by device + direction (per host)
panels.description: SUM of system.disk.io broken down by host.name + device + direction. Honeycomb auto-applies INCREASE to the cumulative counter so values are bytes-per-bucket. Windows device names are PhysicalDrive style (PhysicalDrive0, PhysicalDrive1, ...) — operators with named volumes can clone this panel and group on `mountpoint` instead.

panels.name: Network I/O by interface + direction (per host)
panels.description: SUM of system.network.io broken down by host.name + device + direction. Windows interface names are descriptive (`Ethernet`, `Wi-Fi`, `Loopback Pseudo-Interface 1`); the loopback shows up here but adds little — operators who care about WAN traffic should clone the panel and exclude `Loopback*`.

panels.name: Event Volume by Channel (per host)
panels.description: COUNT of windows-host events broken down by host.name + winlog.channel. Application + System should be roughly comparable on a healthy box; a sudden 10x in System with stable Application is usually a hardware / driver story (look at the next panel for which provider is responsible).

panels.name: Event Volume by Severity (per host)
panels.description: COUNT broken down by host.name + severity_text. The Conduit windowseventlogreceiver maps Event levels to OTel severities: Critical → FATAL, Error → ERROR, Warning → WARN, Information → INFO, Verbose → DEBUG. A baseline-INFO box producing a sustained ERROR rate is typically a service-restart loop visible in the Top Providers panel below.

panels.name: Top Event Providers (last 24h)
panels.description: COUNT broken down by winlog.channel + winlog.provider_name + severity_text, top 25 by count. Drills down 'which application or driver is dominating Event Log volume' — typical answers are noisy services (Schannel, GroupPolicy, MSExchange*), antivirus on a chatty schedule, or third-party agents that log every periodic check at Information level.

panels.name: Recent Errors and Criticals (last 1h)
panels.description: Recent ERROR and FATAL events broken down by winlog.channel + winlog.provider_name + winlog.event_id. The same provider + event_id pair is a stable identifier across versions (Microsoft-Windows-DistributedCOM/10010 means 'DCOM unable to communicate with server' regardless of OS build); operators page on the (provider, event_id) tuple, not the body string.

panels.name: Top Event IDs (provider + event_id, last 24h)
panels.description: COUNT grouped by winlog.provider_name + winlog.event_id, top 25. Distinct event templates as Windows sees them — useful for capacity planning (a single event_id spamming millions of records is the cheapest log to filter at the source) and for spotting accidentally-verbose providers.
